chunk_summary: This section discusses day-by-day breastfeeding adequacy and weight loss triggers in the first two weeks postpartum, focusing on exclusive breastfeeding, weight monitoring, and supplementation guidelines for healthy term neonates.
learning_objectives: Understand the definition and criteria for exclusive breastfeeding in the first two weeks postpartum.
Identify normal weight loss patterns in neonates and recognize red flags for intervention.
Learn evidence-based guidelines for supplementation in healthy term breastfed neonates.
Apply clinical protocols to assess breastfeeding adequacy and manage hyperbilirubinemia.
key_facts: Exclusive breastfeeding is defined as providing only breast milk, with no other liquids or solids, except for medications or supplements as medically indicated.
Neonates typically lose up to 7% of their birth weight in the first 3-5 days, with weight gain expected by day 5-7.
Weight loss exceeding 10% of birth weight is a trigger for further evaluation and possible supplementation.
Supplementation should follow ABM Clinical Protocol #3, prioritizing expressed breast milk or donor milk over formula when possible.
Hyperbilirubinemia management in neonates ≥35 weeks should align with AAP guidelines, considering breastfeeding adequacy as a factor.
Day-by-day monitoring of feeding frequency (8-12 feeds/day) and diaper output (≥6 wet diapers/day by day 5) is critical for assessing adequacy.
reference_quote: "Weight loss exceeding 10% of birth weight in the first week is a red flag for breastfeeding adequacy and requires immediate evaluation and intervention." (ABM Clinical Protocol #2, 2022)
document_key_topics: Neonatal weight loss and red flags
